questions.prompt: At its core, what does an LLM compute?
options: A database lookup over its training documents
A probability distribution over the next token, given the tokens so far
A parse tree of the user's sentence which it then answers logically
A direct mapping from questions to memorized answers
questions.explanation: Everything an LLM does reduces to next-token prediction: given the sequence so far, it outputs a probability for every token in its vocabulary, samples one, appends it, and repeats. There is no retrieval database or symbolic reasoner inside the base mechanism.

questions.prompt: Why does a chat model appear to 'remember' earlier turns of a conversation?
options: The provider stores your session state inside the model between calls
The model's weights are updated after every message you send
Your application re-sends the entire conversation history with each request
Attention layers persist activations between API calls
questions.explanation: The API is stateless: the model has no memory between calls. Chat applications create the illusion of memory by sending the full message history (or a summary of it) on every request — which is also why long conversations get expensive.

questions.prompt: Which training phase turns a raw 'base model' into one that follows instructions?
options: Pre-training on more web text
Supervised fine-tuning on instruction–response pairs (plus preference tuning)
Increasing the context window
Quantization
questions.explanation: Pre-training produces a base model that merely continues text. Supervised fine-tuning on curated instruction→response examples — followed by reinforcement learning from human/AI feedback — is what produces assistant-like, instruction-following behavior.

questions.prompt: Which weaknesses follow directly from how LLMs are built? (Select all that apply.)
options: Unreliable exact arithmetic and character counting
No knowledge of events after the training cutoff
Inability to produce text longer than one sentence
Confidently stated wrong answers (hallucination)
questions.explanation: Token-level pattern matching makes exact arithmetic and character tasks unreliable; the training cutoff bounds knowledge; and the next-token objective rewards plausible-sounding text, which produces confident hallucinations. Long outputs are not a structural problem.

questions.prompt: Roughly how many tokens is 1,000 words of English prose?
options: About 250
About 750
About 1,300
About 4,000
questions.explanation: The rule of thumb is 1 token ≈ 0.75 English words (≈4 characters), so 1,000 words ≈ 1,300 tokens. Code and non-English languages typically tokenize less efficiently.

questions.prompt: Why does asking a model to count the letter 'r' in 'strawberry' often fail?
options: The model's vocabulary doesn't include the letter r
The model sees subword tokens like 'straw'+'berry', not individual characters
Counting requires a context window larger than most models have
Safety filters block character-level questions
questions.explanation: Tokenizers split text into subword chunks. The model never directly observes characters, so character-level tasks (counting letters, reversing strings) are unreliable. The robust fix is tool use — give the model a code interpreter or function.

questions.prompt: What does the 'lost in the middle' effect describe?
options: Models forget the system prompt halfway through generation
Models recall information at the start and end of a long context better than information in the middle
Token prices increase for the middle third of the context window
Streaming responses drop tokens mid-stream
questions.explanation: Empirically, retrieval quality across a long context is U-shaped: content placed at the beginning or end of the prompt is recalled more reliably than content buried in the middle. A big context window is not uniformly reliable attention — placement matters.

questions.prompt: Which statements about context windows are true? (Select all that apply.)
options: The window must hold input and generated output combined
You are billed for the full context you send on every call
Filling the window completely leaves no room for the response
Once a conversation exceeds the window, the model automatically remembers the overflow
questions.explanation: The context window is a combined budget for input plus output, you pay for everything you send each call, and over-filling it truncates the response. Nothing is 'remembered' beyond the window — your application must truncate or summarize.

questions.prompt: What is the key property of a good text embedding space?
options: Vectors are always exactly 1536 dimensions
Semantically similar texts map to nearby points
Each word maps to a unique prime number
Vectors compress text losslessly so the original can be recovered
questions.explanation: Embeddings encode meaning as geometry: similar meanings land close together, so distance measures semantic dissimilarity. Dimensionality varies by model, and embeddings are lossy — you cannot reconstruct the original text.

questions.prompt: You switch your embedding model from model A to model B. What must you do?
options: Nothing — embedding spaces are standardized
Re-embed your entire corpus with model B
Multiply old vectors by a conversion matrix the provider supplies
Only re-embed documents added after the switch
questions.explanation: Vectors from different models (or versions) live in incompatible spaces — comparing a query embedded with model B against documents embedded with model A produces garbage. Changing models means re-embedding everything, so store the model name with your vectors.

questions.prompt: For normalized embedding vectors, cosine similarity is equivalent to what?
options: The dot product
Manhattan distance
Jaccard similarity
Edit distance
questions.explanation: Cosine similarity divides the dot product by the vector norms; with unit-length (normalized) vectors the norms are 1, so cosine similarity is exactly the dot product — and ranking by it matches ranking by Euclidean distance.

questions.prompt: Which inputs tend to embed POORLY for retrieval? (Select all that apply.)
options: A focused paragraph about a single topic
A bare part number like 'SKU-88412-B'
A very long document mixing many unrelated topics
A short, self-contained FAQ answer
questions.explanation: Exact identifiers carry little semantic content for an embedding model (keyword/hybrid search handles them better), and long multi-topic documents dilute into mush — which is why RAG systems chunk documents. Focused, self-contained prose embeds well.

questions.prompt: What does lowering temperature toward 0 actually do?
options: Gives the model more time to think, improving accuracy
Sharpens the sampling distribution toward the highest-probability token
Reduces the number of layers used during inference
Shrinks the context window to save cost
questions.explanation: Temperature rescales the output distribution before sampling. Near 0 it approaches greedy decoding — always the top token. It changes randomness, not capability: the model is neither smarter nor better informed at low temperature.

questions.prompt: Your structured-extraction pipeline returns occasionally truncated JSON. What's the FIRST thing to check?
options: Whether temperature is too high
Whether finish_reason is 'length', meaning max_tokens cut off generation
Whether the model has been deprecated
Whether top_p is set below 0.5
questions.explanation: Truncated output is the signature of hitting the max_tokens cap — the response is cut mid-token and any JSON is broken. Production code should check finish_reason/stop_reason on every call; 'length' means truncation.

questions.prompt: Does setting temperature to 0 eliminate hallucinations?
options: Yes — randomness is the cause of hallucination
No — it only reduces variance; a confidently wrong top token is chosen every time
Yes, but only for models with more than 70B parameters
No, because temperature 0 is not allowed by most APIs
questions.explanation: Hallucination comes from the training objective (plausible text ≠ true text), not from sampling randomness. At temperature 0 the model deterministically picks its top token — if that token is wrong, it's wrong consistently. Grounding and verification reduce hallucination; temperature only stabilizes outputs.

questions.prompt: Which temperature setting best fits generating SQL from natural language?
options: Temperature doesn't apply to SQL
questions.explanation: Code and SQL generation have essentially one correct answer, so you want consistency, not exploration — low temperature (0 to ~0.3) is standard. High temperatures are for brainstorming and creative tasks where diversity is the feature.

questions.prompt: What is the purpose of the 'system' role in the message list?
options: It carries the developer's instructions — persona, rules, format — with higher authority than user messages
It contains operating-system information so the model can run shell commands
It is where the model writes its chain of thought
It is required to be the last message in the list
questions.explanation: The system message (or Anthropic's top-level system parameter) is the developer's channel: it sets behavior, constraints, and output format, and models are trained to weight it above user messages. Module 2 treats it as a versioned software contract.

questions.prompt: A request fails with HTTP 429. What's the correct production response?
options: Immediately retry in a tight loop until it succeeds
Retry with exponential backoff plus jitter, capped at a max attempt count
Switch providers permanently
Lower the temperature and retry
questions.explanation: 429 means rate-limited. Tight-loop retries make the problem worse; exponential backoff with jitter (e.g. ~2^attempt seconds + random) spreads the load and recovers gracefully. Official SDKs often do some of this — know your stack's behavior.

questions.prompt: Which fields should production code inspect on every LLM response? (Select all that apply.)
options: The generated content
usage (input/output token counts)
finish_reason / stop_reason
The model's internal attention weights
questions.explanation: Beyond the content itself, usage powers cost tracking and finish_reason tells you whether output completed naturally, was truncated by max_tokens, wants a tool call, or was filtered. Attention weights aren't exposed by APIs.

questions.prompt: Why pin an exact model version (e.g. 'gpt-4o-2024-08-06') in production rather than a floating alias?
options: Floating aliases cost more per token
Model updates change behavior, and you want to control when that happens — ideally gated by your evals
Pinned versions have larger context windows
Aliases only work in development environments
questions.explanation: Every model version has different behavior, even at temperature 0. Pinning versions means upgrades happen when you choose — after running your eval suite — instead of silently under you.

questions.prompt: What is the recommended procedure for choosing a model for a new task?
options: Pick the current leaderboard #1 and stay on it
Prototype with a frontier model, build a small eval set, then step down tiers until quality breaks
Always use the cheapest model to control costs from day one
Use whichever model has the largest context window
questions.explanation: Establish what's possible with a flagship first, then use your own evals to step down to the cheapest model that still passes your quality bar. Public leaderboards are directional at best; your task-specific evals are the ground truth.

questions.prompt: Which are genuine reasons to choose open-weight models? (Select all that apply.)
options: Data stays inside your infrastructure
They are always more capable than frontier APIs
Freedom to fine-tune and customize
No risk of the provider deprecating the model out from under you
questions.explanation: Open weights buy control: private inference, fine-tuning freedom, and immunity from deprecation, plus potential cost wins at high steady volume. Raw capability still generally favors frontier APIs — that trade-off is the point.

questions.prompt: Where does the deepest provider lock-in actually live?
options: In the HTTP client code, since every API is totally different
In your prompts and evals — prompts tune to a model's behavior, and evals are what make switching safe
In the billing system
In the choice of programming language
questions.explanation: Client APIs have converged (message lists, OpenAI-compatible endpoints everywhere), so the code is easy to port. What's hard is that prompts are tuned to a specific model's quirks — and without an eval suite you can't tell whether a migration regressed quality.

questions.prompt: Why do production systems often use multiple model tiers from the same provider?
options: Providers require it contractually
Cheap models handle routing/extraction at scale while expensive models handle the genuinely hard cases — optimizing cost per unit of quality
Using one model would exceed rate limits
Different tiers share a cache
questions.explanation: The fast/cheap tier is often 10–50× cheaper than the flagship. Routing easy or mechanical work (classification, extraction, triage) to cheap models and reserving the flagship for hard cases is one of the highest-leverage cost optimizations (Module 8).

questions.prompt: Trace the path of one API call: which sequence is correct?
options: Text → tokens → one parallel prefill pass → token-by-token decode → stop at end token / stop sequence / max_tokens
Text → embeddings → database lookup → template fill → response
Text → tokens → decode each input token sequentially → batch output all at once
Text → parse tree → logic engine → renderer
questions.explanation: Input is tokenized, processed in one parallel prefill pass (building the KV cache), then output tokens are decoded one at a time until an end condition. The prefill/decode split explains why input and output tokens have different speeds and prices.

questions.prompt: A teammate says: 'Our 200k-token context window means we can just dump all 150 documents into every prompt.' What's the strongest counterargument?
options: Context windows are measured in characters, not tokens
You'll pay for all of it on every call, and mid-context recall degrades ('lost in the middle') — retrieval of the relevant few documents is cheaper and more reliable
Providers ban prompts over 100k tokens
Documents can't be mixed with conversation history
questions.explanation: Cost scales with tokens sent per call, and attention over huge contexts is not uniformly reliable. Selecting relevant content (RAG, Module 4) usually beats brute-force context stuffing on cost, latency, and accuracy.

questions.prompt: Your support-ticket classifier needs identical labels for identical tickets, run twice. Which configuration is right?
options: Temperature 1.2 for nuanced understanding
Temperature 0 — and ideally a cache, since even T=0 isn't perfectly deterministic
High top_p with high temperature
Maximum max_tokens so the model can think
questions.explanation: Classification wants consistency: temperature 0. But floating-point and batching effects mean T=0 is 'mostly deterministic,' not guaranteed — for hard reproducibility requirements, cache results keyed on input.

questions.prompt: Which practices belong in production LLM code from day one? (Select all that apply.)
options: Logging token usage with request IDs
Checking finish_reason on every response
Hardcoding the API key near the call site for clarity
Retry with exponential backoff on 429/5xx
questions.explanation: Usage logging, finish-reason checks, and backoff retries are baseline hygiene. API keys belong in environment variables or a secrets manager — never in code.

questions.prompt: Why are embeddings computed by a separate model rather than the chat model?
options: Chat models legally cannot produce vectors
Dedicated embedding models are trained specifically to map similar meanings to nearby vectors, and run much faster and cheaper
Embeddings require GPUs while chat models do not
They aren't — the chat model produces embeddings as a side effect of every reply
questions.explanation: Embedding models are purpose-trained (often with contrastive objectives) for semantic similarity, and they're small and cheap — you can embed millions of documents. Chat models optimize for generation, not for a well-structured similarity space.

questions.prompt: Your product takes off in Japan and costs triple the forecast. The most likely cause?
options: Japanese users send more messages
Japanese text tokenizes into 2–4× more tokens than equivalent English, raising per-request cost
The API charges extra for non-ASCII characters
Time-zone-based pricing
questions.explanation: Tokenizer vocabularies are dominated by English; many other languages require several tokens per word. Same content, multiple times the tokens — a classic internationalization cost surprise.

questions.prompt: Which task is the WORST fit for an LLM without tools?
options: Summarizing a meeting transcript
Computing the exact sum of 47 floating-point invoice amounts
Drafting a polite reply to a customer
Translating a paragraph to French
questions.explanation: Exact arithmetic over many values is token-level pattern matching at its weakest — the model will be confidently close-but-wrong. The fix is tool calling (Module 3): let the model call a calculator/code, not do the math in text.

questions.prompt: What's the main reason to keep a task-specific eval set even after launch?
options: Regulators require eval sets for all AI products
It lets you safely adopt new/cheaper models and catch regressions when anything changes — prompts, models, or providers
It improves the model's weights over time
It reduces token costs directly
questions.explanation: Models update, prices drop, and new releases arrive monthly. An eval set turns 'should we switch?' from a leap of faith into an afternoon's measurement — and catches silent regressions. Module 7 is dedicated to this.
